Metadata.Case_Number: Registrar's Appeal Nos 491 and 492 of 2010 (Originating Summons No 879 and 1197 of 2010)

Party_Details.Name: Hau Tau Thong

Facts.Text: Sometime in 2003, the relationship between Hau Tau Thong and his brother Hau Tau Khang began to deteriorate.
At all material times, Hau Tau Thong was the managing director overseeing the business operations of the Companies.
At all material times, Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang were co-signatories of all the Companies' bank accounts.

In 2006, Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang entered into an agreement to keep the Sanur restaurants in operation with a view to selling the business and properties of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd and to carry out an orderly winding up of the companies, referred to as the 2006 Agreement.
After the 2006 Agreement, each of the two brothers accused the other of unreasonable behaviour in managing the winding down process of the Sanur businesses.

Hau Tau Thong is alleged to have failed to use reasonable diligence in filing the Companies' accounts for the financial year ending on 30 June 2008 and sending the said accounts for auditing, resulting in penalties from ACRA.
By 2009, all Sanur restaurants operated by Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd had ceased operations, while both Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang remained as directors.
In October 2009, Hau Tau Thong and his wife started another Indonesian restaurant, Pepes, at the same location at Ngee Ann City.
In August 2010, an application was made for leave to commence a derivative action on behalf of the Companies against Hau Tau Thong for alleged breaches of fiduciary duties.
The Companies allegedly breached a Memorandum of Lease with Frasers Centrepoint involving a covenant to keep the restaurant open daily.
Hau Tau Thong is alleged to have improperly managed operations, including the termination of profitable leases and the closure of the Centrepoint outlet.
Hau Tau Thong is alleged to have failed to use reasonable diligence in paying fees to M/s Spin Creative Pte Ltd, leading to a default judgment against the Companies.
Hau Tau Thong is alleged to have revoked instructions to appoint a solicitor to defend a lawsuit by Cecilia Tan for wrongful dismissal.
Hau Tau Thong is alleged to have deliberately allowed the lease renewal for the flagship restaurant at Ngee Ann City to lapse.
Hau Tau Thong is alleged to have placed himself in a position of conflict by incorporating Ochacha (Singapore) Pte Ltd and Simple Spice Pte Ltd without disclosure.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd can enforce a statutory right to inspect the full set of corporate accounting records when those records are needed to respond to allegations of financial and operational mismanagement in a proposed derivative action.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 199(3) of the Companies Act, corporate records are at all times open to inspection by directors. This right is a legal incident of the office and is generally absolute, meaning it can be exercised without stating reasons unless there is clear proof that the inspection is sought for an improper purpose unconnected to director functions or to cause substantial harm to the company.
Party_Details.Application: Hau Tau Thong, a director and equal shareholder, seeks access to the full accounting records, including cash deposit slips and cheque book records, that were created during his tenure as managing director. A forensic report commissioned by his co-director, Hau Tau Khang, alleges that over $153,000 in cash sales was not deposited into bank accounts. Hau Tau Thong maintains that inspection of the original records is necessary to verify his management of the business and prepare for the proposed derivative action. The companies and co-director resist, arguing that the inspection is for a private purpose to obtain 'ammunition' for his defense and to bypass the standard discovery process.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director's statutory right of inspection flows directly from the office and is essential for the discharge of fiduciary duties. The burden of proof to displace this right rests on the party opposing inspection, who must demonstrate an ulterior purpose or intent to injure the company. Using inspection to obtain information to clarify financial irregularities or respond to claims of mismanagement does not constitute an improper private purpose, as it is in the company's interest to resolve such disputes on an accurate factual basis. Furthermore, since the documents are company property and would eventually be discoverable in litigation, providing access earlier to a sitting director is consistent with the statutory framework. Absent clear proof of intended harm, the right of a director to remain informed about the company's affairs through its accounts remains enforceable.

Metadata.Case_Number: Registrar's Appeal Nos 491 and 492 of 2010 (Originating Summons No 879 and 1197 of 2010)

Party_Details.Name: Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and another (Sanur Holding Pte Ltd)

Facts.Text: By 2003, Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd were companies engaged in operating a chain of restaurants serving Indonesian cuisine under the trade name 'Sanur'.
By 2003, Hau Tau Thong and Hau Tau Khang were co-directors and equal shareholders of Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd.
In 2006, the companies operated under an agreement between the two director-shareholders with the objective of selling the business and carrying out an orderly winding up.
For the financial year ending 30 June 2008, the companies did not file accounts or send them for auditing within the statutory timeframe, leading to ACRA penalties.
By 2008, the companies faced a default judgment from Spin Creative Pte Ltd due to unpaid service fees.
By 2009, all Sanur restaurants operated by the companies had ceased operations.
In September 2009, director Hau Tau Khang obtained possession of the keys to the companies' documentation cabinets containing accounting and corporate records.
A forensic examination report by Stone Forest Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd stated that cash sales totalling S$153,525.45 were not deposited into bank accounts.
Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd maintained DBS cheque book records, and daily cash deposit slips for outlets at Causeway Point and Centrepoint.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the corporate entities can lawfully resist a co-director's exercise of the statutory right of inspection on the ground that the director intends to use the corporate accounts for personal defense in a derivative action.
Party_Details.Rule: A company may resist a director's statutory right to inspect accounts under section 199(3) only by providing clear and positive proof that the inspection is sought for a purpose wholly unconnected with the performance of director duties or is intended to cause detriment to the company.
Party_Details.Application: Sanur Indonesian Restaurant Pte Ltd and Sanur Holding Pte Ltd experienced significant lease terminations and operational closures. Following a forensic review of documentation cabinets, the companies identified cash sales discrepancies. Hau Tau Thong, as a director, seeks full access to the accounting records used for the forensic review to verify his own management. The companies resist, contending that the information will be used as 'ammunition' for his defense in the pending derivative proceedings and that this constitutes an improper private purpose.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The statutory right of inspection support's a director's duties of care and diligence and the general need to be informed about the company's financial status. The exception to this right requires clear evidence that the inspection would be abused to cause substantial harm. Using access to clarify a company's financial position in the face of mismanagement allegations is not necessarily a purpose unconnected to directorial functions. Because the entities are still holding records and have sitting directors with equal status, the companies cannot unilaterally withhold these records based on the existence of a shareholder dispute. Unless the companies provide proof of an intent to injure the corporate interest, the statutory right of the director to inspect corporate accounts remains paramount.

Metadata.Case_Number: Registrar's Appeal Nos 491 and 492 of 2010 (Originating Summons No 879 and 1197 of 2010)

Party_Details.Name: Hau Tau Khang

Facts.Text: Sometime in 2003, the relationship between the brothers began to deteriorate.
Hau Tau Khang and Hau Tau Thong were co-directors and co-signatories of all the Companies' bank accounts.
The brothers agreed in 2006 to sell the business and properties and carry out an orderly winding up.
Hau Tau Khang terminated the office manager in September 2009 and took possession of the keys to the corporate documentation cabinets.
Hau Tau Khang engaged Stone Forest Corporate Advisory Pte Ltd to conduct a forensic examination of the accounts to investigate alleged cash discrepancies.
The forensic report identified a total of S$153,525.45 in cash sales not deposited into the companies' bank accounts.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a co-director who has obtained exclusive control over company records can prevent his co-director from accessing those same records for the purpose of addressing allegations of financial misconduct.
Party_Details.Rule: A director's right of inspection is a statutory incident of the office and is generally enforceable between co-directors of equal status. A co-director seeking to block access must prove that the requesting director is acting in bad faith or for a purpose wholly unrelated to his corporate role.
Party_Details.Application: Hau Tau Khang reviewed the companies' accounts, identified discrepancies, and initiated a forensic audit. He then used this information to seek leave for a derivative action against Hau Tau Thong. When Hau Tau Thong requested access to the full records to verify the discrepancies, Hau Tau Khang resisted, alleging that the co-director's purpose was purely to build a personal defense and circumvent litigation discovery rules.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A sitting director's right to be informed about company affairs through its accounts is a fundamental aspect of their role. A co-director cannot unilaterally withhold these records based on personal litigation strategy or a desire to maintain an informational advantage in a dispute. While the court discourages attempts to obtain undiscoverable documents through the statutory right of inspection, these documents are corporate records that would inherently be discoverable. Clarifying the company's financial position in response to a forensic audit is a legitimate exercise of a director's functions. Unless there is clear evidence of an intent to injure the company or use the data for a purpose entirely outside the director's responsibilities, the right of inspection cannot be denied by a co-director.
